title: Spinning fine merino on a top-whorl drop spindle
subtitle: Drafting control for a short, slick fibre on a light spindle
excerpt: Merino is short-stapled (4-7 cm) and very smooth, which makes it slide apart before enough twist has entered the drafting zone. A light top-whorl spindle of 20-30 g, careful predrafting, and a short-forward draw kept well within the staple length give a fine, even singles suited to 2-ply lace or fingering weight.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. Drop-spindle technique on fine wools is covered in Abby Franquemont, Respect the Spindle (referenced for substance, not quoted). Merino's fibre characteristics (staple 4-7 cm, 15-24 microns) are documented across commercial top suppliers and breed guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of an individual fibre from end to end, measured in centimetres. Fine merino staples run 4-7 cm. Because the staple is short, the hand drafting the fibre must stay very close to the drafting zone so no fibre is unsupported between the pinch gate and the twist.

Drawing roving apart by hand before spinning to open the fibre structure and reduce it to a density the spindle can handle. Merino benefits from careful predrafting because commercial merino roving is often compressed, and forcing a compacted zone through a spindle draft produces slubs and breaks.

A single strand of yarn before plying. A fine merino singles on a drop spindle should feel smooth and slightly firm with active Z-twist. It will kink back on itself if you allow a length to go slack: that kinking confirms the twist is alive and the fibre is held together.

Wraps per inch: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler over one inch (2.5 cm) without overlapping or stretching. A fine merino singles spun on a light drop spindle typically lands at 28-35 WPI, plying to a lace or fingering weight at 20-28 WPI as a 2-ply.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Pulling and predrafting merino roving releases very fine fibre particles. Work near an open window; damp-mop the floor below you after the session.

content.text: Fine merino on a drop spindle is not a forgiving combination for a spinner who is still developing drafting control. The fibre is short-stapled (4-7 cm), which gives the twist very little length of fibre to grip before the drafting zone moves on. It is also very smooth: the scales on merino lie flat, so when you apply tension at the wrong moment, the fibres slide past each other and the draft falls apart. The fix is not strength but precision: a light spindle, thorough
predrafting
, and a short-forward draw kept strictly within the
staple length
of the fibre.
The reward for getting it right is a yarn that is noticeably soft against the skin, with good elasticity and a fine, even diameter. A
singles
WPI
plied to a 2-ply gives a fingering-weight yarn well suited to fine shawls, socks, and lightweight mittens.
What you need

items.name: Top-whorl drop spindle, 20-30 g weight (lighter spindles add twist faster and suit fine fibres)
Niddy-noddy for winding the finished singles into a hank
Soft tape measure for measuring staple length

content.text: Preparing the merino
Step 1: Run the staple-length test

Pull a small tuft from the roving and gently draw its two ends apart. The fibres should slip past each other smoothly, releasing without snapping. If they break rather than slide, the top is over-processed and the fibres are fractured: treat it as a short-staple fibre and keep your drafting hands very close together throughout. Pull a few individual fibres free and lay them flat against a soft tape measure to check the actual length. Fine merino runs 4-7 cm. Write this number on a piece of paper and keep it visible at your drafting position. Your hands must stay closer together than this number at all times during the draft.
Step 2: Predraft the roving

Pull 40-50 cm of roving from the ball and split it lengthways once or twice until it is roughly as thick as a pencil. Hold the split length between both hands, 5-6 cm apart (slightly less than the staple length), and draw your hands slowly apart along the length. Feel for resistance: smooth is correct; a sudden catch means a compressed zone that needs patient work at a shorter hand spacing. Predraft the full working length before spinning. Set the predrafted lengths in loose coils; do not compress them back into a ball.
Spinning with the drop spindle
Step 3: Set up the spindle

Tie a leader of about 50 cm onto the shaft below the whorl with a half-hitch. Bring the leader up, over the rim of the whorl, and around the shaft above the whorl with another half-hitch to stop the spindle unwinding mid-draft. A top-whorl spindle, set up this way, adds twist quickly from a short drop; the 20-30 g weight is light enough to stay aloft on a fine singles without pulling it apart.
Step 4: Join and begin the short-forward draw

Overlap the predrafted roving with the top of the leader by 5 cm. Hold this overlap between the thumb and forefinger of the front hand (the pinch gate). Spin the spindle clockwise by rolling it down your thigh: it falls and spins, adding Z-twist. While the spindle turns, draw the back hand backward 4-5 cm, then release the front pinch and allow the waiting twist to run into the drafted section. Close the front pinch again immediately to stop the twist running further. Advance the front hand by 4-5 cm, and repeat.

The key dimension is the 4-5 cm draw: shorter than the staple length of 4-7 cm so every fibre spans at least two points of contact at all times. If you draw further than the staple, the unsupported section of roving has nothing to hold it together once the pinch gate opens.
Step 5: Check twist build per draw

After every 30-40 cm of spinning, let the spindle hang still and look at the yarn in your drafting zone. It should show 8-12 visible spirals per 2.5 cm of length for a fingering-weight singles. Fewer than eight spirals and the singles will drift apart when you wind it onto the spindle shaft; more than twelve and it will be over-twisted and wiry. Re-spin the spindle clockwise for a few seconds to add twist before the next draw if the count is low.
Step 6: Wind on before the spindle stops

When the spindle drops to within 15 cm of the floor, or when it slows and begins to reverse direction, stop drafting. Unhook the half-hitches from the whorl and wind the spun yarn onto the shaft in a cone shape, leaving 20 cm free above the whorl to rejoin. Rehook the half-hitches, re-spin the spindle, and continue. Merino singles is fragile before it is plied: allow the spindle to reverse direction (adding S-twist) and the singles will unwind and break at the thinnest point.
Step 7: Wind onto the niddy-noddy

When the spindle shaft is full, wind the singles onto the niddy-noddy to make a hank. Tie the hank loosely in three places with waste yarn. The singles at this stage is active: it will kink the moment it is released from tension. Keep the hank tied until plying.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The draft falls apart repeatedly, especially in fine merino
items.cause: The draw is longer than the staple length of the fibre, leaving an unsupported zone with nothing to hold the fibres together when the pinch gate opens.
items.fix: Shorten the draw to 3-4 cm. Keep both hands closer together throughout. If breaks continue, the predraft was not thorough enough: compressed zones in the roving create sudden thin spots that break apart under spindle tension. Predraft the whole working length again before continuing.

items.problem: The spindle reverses direction mid-draft and the singles unwinds
items.cause: The spindle slowed to a stop before enough yarn was wound on. The single half-hitch on the whorl is not holding the twist against reversal.
items.fix: Wind on more frequently: at the first sign that the spindle is slowing, stop drafting and wind on. Check the half-hitches are secure. A very light spindle (under 15 g) runs out of momentum faster; try a 25-30 g spindle for merino if a lighter one keeps reversing.

items.problem: The singles is consistent in the first 10 cm but becomes thicker further along
items.cause: The back hand is drifting further from the front hand as the session continues, increasing the draw length beyond the staple.
items.fix: Mark a 4-5 cm reference on your forearm with a pen or hold a small piece of card with the measurement written on it. Check your hand spacing against the reference every few draws until the distance is automatic.

items.problem: The finished singles is fuzzy rather than smooth
items.cause: Some fibres escaped the drafting zone at an angle. This is partly the nature of merino at fine weights, but excessive fuzz means the pinch gate is releasing too early before the twist has fully entered the drafted section.
items.fix: Hold the front pinch for a count of one full beat before releasing. Do not rush the advance. Accept that a drop-spindle merino singles will have slightly more halo than a wheel-spun one: the spindle's intermittent twist is less consistent than continuous wheel treadling.
